questions.question: Why is it necessary to use semaphores in conjunction with shared memory? (2-3 marks)
expectedPoints: Shared memory allows direct, concurrent access by multiple processes.
This concurrent access can lead to race conditions where the outcome depends on the order of execution.
Semaphores provide the necessary synchronization (e.g., mutual exclusion) to prevent these race conditions and ensure data consistency.

questions.question: Explain the producer-consumer problem and how it can be solved using shared memory and a set of three semaphores. Describe the role of each semaphore. (5-10 marks)
expectedPoints: Definition: One or more producer processes generate data and place it in a buffer, while one or more consumer processes remove and use that data.
Problem: Need to synchronize so producers don't overfill the buffer and consumers don't try to consume from an empty buffer, all while preventing race condition during buffer access.
Shared Memory: Used to create the shared buffer that both producers and consumers access.
Semaphore 1 (mutex): A binary semaphore used for mutual exclusion to ensure only one process accesses the buffer at a time.
Semaphore 2 (empty): A counting semaphore that tracks the number of empty slots in the buffer. Initialized to the buffer size (N).
Semaphore 3 (full): A counting semaphore that tracks the number of full slots in the buffer. Initialized to 0.
Producer Algorithm: Wait(empty) -> Wait(mutex) -> Produce -> Signal(mutex) -> Signal(full).
Consumer Algorithm: Wait(full) -> Wait(mutex) -> Consume -> Signal(mutex) -> Signal(empty).
